skill.name: dcg
skill.description: Destructive Command Guard documents a Claude Code hook that blocks selected destructive shell and git commands before execution.
skill.summary: Document and configure a Claude Code hook for blocking destructive commands.
skill.author: Dicklesworthstone

security_audit.summary: Static analysis raised many command, filesystem, and weak-crypto alerts. Most destructive command matches are documentation examples for a guard tool, but the installation section confirms a high-risk remote script execution pattern, including a sudo variant. No prompt injection evidence was found in SKILL.md.

high_findings.title: Remote Shell Installer Piped To Bash
high_findings.description: The installation instructions pipe a remote GitHub script directly into bash and provide a sudo bash system-wide variant. This creates a supply-chain and privilege escalation risk because users execute network-delivered code without local review or integrity verification.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is explicit and includes a privileged sudo execution path. The context is installation guidance, so legitimate intent is possible, but marketplace users would be asked to run unverified remote code.

medium_findings.title: Hook Can Be Bypassed By Environment Variable
medium_findings.description: The documentation defines DCG_BYPASS=1 as a full bypass for the guard. This is a legitimate escape hatch, but it weakens the security control and should be clearly warned to users.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The bypass is documented directly and has clear security impact. It is not malicious by itself, but it materially limits the protection model.

medium_findings.title: Default-Allow Guard Leaves Coverage Gaps
medium_findings.description: The skill states that unrecognized commands are allowed by default and that commands inside scripts are not inspected. This limits protection against novel destructive commands and indirect execution through scripts.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The limitations are explicitly documented and align with the tool threat model. They are legitimate design tradeoffs, but users may overestimate the guard coverage.

low_findings.title: Destructive Command Examples Are Documentation Context
low_findings.description: Many static findings identify rm, git reset, force push, disk, database, and cloud destruction strings. These appear in tables and examples describing what the guard blocks, not as executable skill code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The surrounding prose repeatedly frames these commands as blocked examples. This supports dismissing most blocker alerts as false positives for executable behavior.

low_findings.title: Hidden Configuration File References Are Expected Setup Paths
low_findings.description: The references to home-directory configuration files point to documented DCG and Claude Code configuration locations. They do not show secret harvesting or unauthorized filesystem access.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The paths are used for user-directed configuration. I did not find evidence that the skill reads, copies, or exfiltrates hidden files.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Lack Supporting Context
low_findings.description: Static analysis reported many weak cryptography alerts, but the reviewed content describes command guarding, pattern names, and performance. No evidence found of cryptographic operations in SKILL.md.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file is Markdown documentation, and the cited contexts do not contain crypto use. The static weak-crypto results are likely keyword collisions or context-free matches.

dangerous_patterns.title: curl Piped Directly To Bash
dangerous_patterns.description: The quick install command downloads a script from GitHub and executes it immediately through bash. Users cannot verify the script before execution from the documented command.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The install commands plainly combine network download and shell execution. This is a well-known risky installer pattern.

dangerous_patterns.title: Remote Installer Executed With sudo
dangerous_patterns.description: The system-wide installation variant pipes the same remote script into sudo bash. If the remote script or delivery path is compromised, it could execute with elevated privileges.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The sudo variant is explicit and gives the remote installer privileged execution. This is the highest-confidence dangerous pattern in the file.

content.value_statement: AI coding agents can accidentally run commands that delete work or rewrite history. This skill documents DCG, a Claude Code hook that blocks selected destructive shell and git commands before execution.
actual_capabilities: Explains how DCG blocks selected destructive Bash commands through a Claude Code PreToolUse hook.
Documents blocked git operations such as hard reset, force push, destructive checkout, and stash deletion.
Documents filesystem protection for recursive forced deletion outside temporary directories.
Describes optional pattern packs for databases, containers, Kubernetes, cloud tools, infrastructure, and system commands.
Shows configuration for enabling packs and wiring DCG into Claude Code settings.
Explains exit codes, manual testing, block messages, and known threat model limits.
limitations: The skill contains documentation only; implementation source is installed from an external repository.
The documented quick install uses remote shell execution and should be reviewed before use.
The guard allows unrecognized commands by default, so coverage is not complete.
It does not inspect commands hidden inside scripts or protect against malicious users.

use_cases.title: Protect Active Repositories
use_cases.description: Use DCG to reduce accidental loss from destructive git or shell commands during AI-assisted coding sessions.

use_cases.title: Standardize Agent Safety Rules
use_cases.description: Document shared command-blocking policies for teams that let AI agents work in local repositories.

use_cases.title: Review Hook-Based Safeguards
use_cases.description: Evaluate DCG behavior, bypasses, and coverage gaps before adding it to a secure development environment.

prompt_templates.title: Check Basic Fit
prompt_templates.prompt: Explain whether DCG is useful for my Claude Code workflow. Focus on the commands it blocks and the risks it does not cover.

prompt_templates.title: Configure Claude Code Hook
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me configure DCG as a Claude Code PreToolUse hook. Include the settings path, expected hook command, and restart steps.

prompt_templates.title: Assess Command Coverage
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this list of commands from our workflow and classify which DCG would likely allow, block, or miss based on the documented behavior.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Deployment Risk
prompt_templates.prompt: Evaluate the DCG installation and threat model for a locked-down engineering environment. Identify risks from remote installers, bypass variables, and default-allow behavior.

output_examples.input: Can DCG stop an accidental git reset during a Claude Code session?
output_examples.output: Yes. The documented guard blocks hard reset patterns and returns a denial message before the Bash command executes.

output_examples.input: What should I review before installing DCG?
output: Review the external installer source before execution.
Avoid running remote install scripts with sudo unless you trust the source.
Confirm the hook configuration is active after restarting Claude Code.

output_examples.input: Does DCG protect against every destructive command?
output_examples.output: No. The documentation states that unrecognized commands are allowed and script contents are not inspected.

best_practices: Review the installer script locally before running any curl-to-shell command.
Treat DCG as a last line of defense, not a replacement for backups and git hygiene.
Keep bypass settings restricted and document when they are used.
anti_patterns: Do not run the sudo installer variant without verifying the remote script first.
Do not assume DCG blocks commands inside arbitrary scripts.
Do not rely on default rules for production or high-risk infrastructure workflows.

faq.question: What does this skill help with?
faq.answer: It documents DCG, a Claude Code hook that blocks selected destructive Bash and git commands before execution.

faq.question: Is this safe to publish without warnings?
faq.answer: No. The documented installer pipes a remote script into bash, including a sudo option, so users need a strong warning.

faq.question: Does DCG block every dangerous command?
faq.answer: No. The documentation says unrecognized commands are allowed by default and commands inside scripts are not inspected.

faq.question: Can users bypass DCG?
faq.answer: Yes. The documentation describes DCG_BYPASS=1 and manual execution as escape paths.

faq.question: Does the skill include executable source code?
faq.answer: No evidence found in this package. The reviewed file is SKILL.md documentation for installing and configuring an external tool.

faq.question: Which tools does it target?
faq.answer: It is primarily written for Claude Code hooks, while the marketplace metadata also lists Claude and Codex compatibility.
